ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (fish gelatin) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for fish-scale collagen jelly as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bottled serving of sea collagen jelly is approximately 200 g, equivalent to a small jar as typically sold at Singaporean Chinese seafood restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Fish gelatin / collagen jelly — estimated from USDA FoodData Central: Gelatin, dry powder, unsweetened (FDC ID 169596) and peer-reviewed literature on fish collagen composition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central; supplemented by published fish collagen research
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for fish-scale collagen jelly exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated from USDA gelatin (dry powder, unsweetened) diluted to a prepared jelly state (~6–8 g gelatin per 100 g water-based jelly), consistent with published fish collagen jelly preparation ratios. Protein content reflects hydrolysed collagen peptides. Carbohydrate and fat are negligible in plain preparations. Sodium reflects a lightly seasoned restaurant preparation. Values represent a moderate-confidence estimate for a plain-to-lightly-seasoned fish-scale collagen jelly.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein-dominant gelatin-based foods — analogous estimate; reference: University of Sydney GI Database, plain gelatin dessert analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fish-scale collagen jelly. The food is predominantly protein (collagen peptides) with negligible carbohydrate (~0.3 g per 100 g). Plain gelatin-based foods with minimal carbohydrate content are classified as low GI by the University of Sydney GI Database convention. GI is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this food contains no meaningful glycemic carbohydrates. GL per 200 g serving = (1 × 0.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 0.6, rounded to 0.6.

hypertension.guidance: A 200 g bottled serving provides approximately 240 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should monitor cumulative sodium intake, particularly if the jelly is prepared with soy sauce or salted seasonings, and should prefer unseasoned or lightly seasoned versions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate and very low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fish-scale collagen jelly contains approximately 0.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g and has a negligible glycemic load, making it a suitable low-GI protein-rich food for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided it is not served with high-sugar dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat and high collagen protein content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fish-scale collagen jelly is very low in saturated fat (~0.05 g per 100 g) and contains no trans fat, making it a heart-friendly protein source; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of sodium from seasoning and choose low-sodium preparations.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sea Collagen Jelly, known in Chinese as 鱼鳞冻 (Yú Lín Dòng, literally 'fish scale jelly'), is a traditional Chinese cold dish made by simmering fish scales — typically from large freshwater or marine fish — in water until the natural collagen dissolves, then allowing the liquid to set into a firm, translucent jelly upon chilling. The dish is popular in Chinese home cooking and is served at Chinese seafood restaurants in Singapore, including hot-pot style establishments. The texture is smooth, firm, and slightly gelatinous, with a mild savoury flavour that is often enhanced with vinegar, soy sauce, sesame oil, ginger, or chilli. Nutritionally, fish-scale collagen jelly is a high-protein, very low-carbohydrate, low-fat food. Its protein is predominantly hydrolysed collagen (glycine, proline, hydroxyproline), which differs from complete dietary protein in amino acid profile but is valued in traditional Chinese medicine and modern wellness contexts for skin, joint, and connective tissue support. With approximately 28 kcal and 6.5 g protein per 100 g, it is a low-calorie, protein-rich food suitable for weight management. The glycemic index is effectively zero due to negligible carbohydrate content, making it appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content varies by preparation; bottled restaurant versions may contain 100–150 mg sodium per 100 g. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free, and is halal when prepared without non-halal additives.

Is sea collagen jelly suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for fish-scale collagen jelly exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA gelatin (dry powder) diluted to prepared jelly state, cross-referenced with published fish collagen composition literature. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and consistent with known food science, but direct analytical data for this specific product is not available.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central gelatin entry (FDC ID 169596) adjusted for prepared jelly dilution (~6–8 g dry gelatin per 100 g jelly), consistent with standard fish-scale collagen jelly preparation. Vitamin A, B-vitamins (B1, B2, B3, B6, B12), folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, and selenium are set to -1 as they are not reported in the gelatin source entry and are not expected to be present in meaningful quantities in a plain collagen jelly preparation. Vitamin C is set to 0.0 as collagen jelly contains no ascorbic acid; this is confirmed zero per the USDA gelatin source. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 as fish-scale collagen jelly contains no trans fats by nature (naturally zero for this food type). Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as collagen jelly contains no dietary fibre (naturally zero — collagen is a structural protein, not a plant-based fibre source). Glycemic index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this food contains no meaningful glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category is low per University of Sydney GI Database convention for negligible-carbohydrate protein foods. GL per 200 g serving calculated as (1 × 0.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.006, reported as 0.6 for the full 200 g serving. The restaurant source is Hai Kah Lang at Funan Mall, Singapore (GrabFood listing). Price SGD 13.32 per bottle. FoodOn match FOODON_03315873 is closest parent (fish gelatin food product); no exact term exists. FoodEx2 code A0CKP is closest parent (fish gelatin). SNOMED CT has no suitable concept. Follow-up: direct laboratory analysis of this specific product would improve confidence to high.
